text: We present a new class of transition metal-organic frameworks based on a redox active, semiquinoid ligand and report the synthesis and characterization of the open circuit potential reduction product (NBu4)2Fe(III)2(dhbq)3, a network solid containing Fe(III) centers bridged by paramagnetic semiquinoid radical anions and having a room temperature conductivity of 0.16 ± 0.01 S/cm. The high room temperature conductivity of this material, one of the highest reported for a MOF, along with clear indication of ligand redox activity, the presence of long range electronic coupling indicated by temperature dependent behavior in the slow-scan cyclic voltammograms, and temperature dependent conductivity and magnetic susceptibility, all combined strongly support the presence of ligand mixed-valency. The presence of ligand mixed-valency activity has been confirmed for the first time to our knowledge using UV-vis-NIR diffuse reflectance spectroscopy and shown to be responsible for the high room temperature conductivity of the solid. Reducing the stoichiometry, in the form of the reduced material Na0.9(NBu4)1.8Fe(III)2(dhbq)3, leads to different electronic and magnetic properties, offering further tuning of the properties of transition metal-organic frameworks through the manipulation of ligand redox activity. In general, our results indicate that transition metal–semiquinoid frameworks hold considerable promise as building blocks for the design of porous solids that can be tailored to possess a wide range of electronic functionalities.
